title: Valid Anagram
code: public bool IsAnagram(string s, string t)

frames.caption: Lengths match (7 = 7), so an anagram is still possible. Set up a 26-slot count array, all zero.
frames.note: First reject on length: two strings of different lengths can never be anagrams, so this O(1) check bails before any work. The freq array is stackalloc int[26] — a fixed buffer on the stack indexed by c − 'a', giving O(1) space independent of input size. Anagrams are exactly strings with the same multiset of characters, so all we need to verify is that every letter's count balances to zero.

frames.caption: i=0: s[0]='a' → freq['a']++ = 1; t[0]='n' → freq['n']-- = -1.
frames.note: Increment the slot for s[i] and decrement the slot for t[i] in the SAME iteration. Because both strings have equal length, the array stays balanced as a running tally: a positive slot means s has a surplus of that letter so far, a negative slot means t does. The slot is freq[c - 'a'] — subtracting 'a' turns a lowercase letter into a perfect 0..25 index, a hash with no collisions, no boxing, and O(1) lookup. This single counting pass is what replaces the O(n log n) two-sorts approach.

frames.caption: i=1: s[1]='n' → freq['n']++ = 0; t[1]='a' → freq['a']-- = 0.
frames.note: Increment the slot for s[i] and decrement the slot for t[i] in the SAME iteration. Because both strings have equal length, the array stays balanced as a running tally: a positive slot means s has a surplus of that letter so far, a negative slot means t does. The slot is freq[c - 'a'] — subtracting 'a' turns a lowercase letter into a perfect 0..25 index, a hash with no collisions, no boxing, and O(1) lookup. This single counting pass is what replaces the O(n log n) two-sorts approach.

frames.note: Increment the slot for s[i] and decrement the slot for t[i] in the SAME iteration. Because both strings have equal length, the array stays balanced as a running tally: a positive slot means s has a surplus of that letter so far, a negative slot means t does. The slot is freq[c - 'a'] — subtracting 'a' turns a lowercase letter into a perfect 0..25 index, a hash with no collisions, no boxing, and O(1) lookup. frames.caption: Every slot returned to zero, so s and t hold the same letters with the same counts → they are anagrams. Return true.
frames.note: The foreach scans all 26 slots: any nonzero value would mean a surplus (s had a letter t lacked) or a deficit (the reverse), so a single nonzero slot is an immediate "not an anagram". Here all five touched slots — and the 21 untouched ones — are zero, proving the multisets are identical. Total cost: O(n) time, O(1) space, a clear win over sorting both strings.
